meta.label: Nigerian
meta.name: Nigerian
meta.description: Bold, aromatic West African cooking centered on pepper bases, onion-forward stews, deep savory umami, and paired starches (swallows, rice, yams). Emphasizes layered cooking: bloom aromatics, build pepper base, fortify with proteins and umami, then balance with leafy greens and bright condiments.
meta.subtitle: Pepper bases • Stews & soups • Rice dishes • Swallows • Smoky grilled street foods
heritageNotes: Nigerian cuisine varies widely by region and ethnicity (Yoruba, Igbo, Hausa, Edo, Efik/Ibibio and more). This profile models cross-cutting patterns commonly used in home cooking and diaspora kitchens.
Many iconic dishes rely on palm oil and dried seafood for signature flavor; this profile provides structured substitutions when those are unavailable.

source.attribution: Structured from common Nigerian home-cooking patterns (pepper base, stew-and-starch pairing, dried seafood umami, greens integration).
source.author: SSA
source.notes: This profile is deterministic/planner-oriented: it encodes pantry tiers, dish archetypes, substitutions, and pairing rules for SSA meal planning and recipe adaptation.

overlapsWith.reason: Shared pepper-forward stews, fried plantain, and savory spice layering in diaspora cooking.
Shared stew-building logic (aromatics → fat bloom → sauce base → protein → simmer), though different spice sets.

subprofiles.name: Pepper Soup
subprofiles.signatureNotes: Clear, aromatic broth with pepper-soup spice blend; often fish or goat; served with yam/plantain or as a restorative soup.

subprofiles.name: Jollof & Party Rice
subprofiles.signatureNotes: Tomato-pepper rice with deep smoky notes (party jollof) and a rich base built from blended peppers, onions, and tomato paste.

subprofiles.name: Soups + Swallow Pairing
subprofiles.signatureNotes: Thick, intensely flavored soups (egusi, ogbono, okra, efo/vegetable, oha/okazi) designed to be eaten with swallow (pounded yam, eba, fufu).

subprofiles.name: Street Foods & Grills
subprofiles.signatureNotes: Suya-spiced grills, fried snacks (puff puff, akara), and quick pepper sauces with crisp onions/tomatoes.

regionHints: Yoruba (southwest): efo riro, amala + ewedu/gbegiri, obe ata
Igbo (southeast): oha soup, bitterleaf soup, ofe onugbu
Efik/Ibibio (south-south): afang/edikang ikong greens, seafood-forward soups

notes: IDs in this file follow ^[a-z0-9_.-]+$ to satisfy schema constraints.
Ingredient/equipment IDs are SSA-canonical placeholders and should map to your ingredient/equipment catalogs (or alias maps) for best results.
Where multiple real-world names exist (e.g., iru/locust beans, ogiri), this profile uses normalized IDs and lists alternate names in search hints.

plannerDefaults.weeknightBias: Stew bases can be batch-cooked; weeknight meals often remix pre-cooked stew/soup with fresh starch or quick sides.

batchCookingBias.notes: Cook pepper base and proteins ahead; finish with greens, okra, or thickener on serving day.

signatureNotes: deep savory umami from dried seafood/fermented seasoning
rich red stew base from peppers + tomato + oil bloom
greens integrated into sauces/soups rather than served plain

tier0_minimum.description: Basic Nigerian-forward cooking without specialty items; relies on onion/pepper base and common oils/spices.

tier1_core.description: Core Nigerian pantry for signature flavor: crayfish, palm oil, key thickeners, and basic swallows.

tier2_traditional.description: Traditional/regional essentials: iru/locust beans, specialty greens, and spice blends for pepper soup and suya.

tier3_specialty.description: Specialty items for authenticity and regional depth (diaspora availability varies).

notes: Many dishes start with blended peppers + onion (pepper base) cooked in oil until reduced and sweetened.
Palm oil is often bloomed with aromatics/seasonings; use controlled heat to avoid scorching.
Greens are frequently added near the end to preserve color/texture.

dishArchetypes.label: Soup + Swallow
dishArchetypes.description: Thick, intensely flavored soup designed to be eaten with swallow (eba, pounded yam, semo, fufu).

dishArchetypes.label: Stew + Rice
dishArchetypes.description: Reduced pepper-tomato stew served with plain rice or integrated into jollof-style rice.

dishArchetypes.label: Pepper Soup
dishArchetypes.description: Clear, aromatic, spicy broth using pepper soup spice blend; commonly goat, fish, or chicken.

dishArchetypes.label: Suya + Sides
dishArchetypes.description: Suya-spiced grilled meats (or oven-roasted) served with onions, tomatoes, and quick pepper sauces.

dishArchetypes.label: Beans + Fried Plantain
dishArchetypes.description: Beans cooked with aromatics and oil served alongside sweet fried plantain; often with pepper sauce.

dishArchetypes.label: Akara Breakfast
dishArchetypes.description: Fried bean cakes with pepper and onion; eaten for breakfast or snacks.

avoid_or_limit.label: High added sugar
avoid_or_limit.reason: Most Nigerian stews/soups are savory; sweetness comes primarily from reduced peppers and onions.

avoid_or_limit.label: Heavy dairy
avoid_or_limit.reason: Not typical in core Nigerian stew/soup patterns (though modern fusion exists).

proteinSubstitutions.notes: Goat can be swapped with beef/lamb for similar stew depth; chicken cooks faster and yields a lighter broth.
Stockfish can be hard to find; smoked/dried fish or dried shrimp provide similar savory backbone.

sideSubstitutions.notes: If eba (gari) is unavailable, semo or fufu-style swallows work; boiled/pounded yam is a common swap.
Yam can be substituted with plantain or potatoes for boil/roast sides; rice for stew pairing.

thickenerSubstitutions.notes: Egusi is a ground seed thickener; ground pumpkin seed can approximate body; regional thickeners also work.
Ogbono adds slick viscosity; okra can mimic viscosity, though flavor differs.

notes: Palm oil substitutes: peanut/vegetable oil plus a small amount of smoked paprika can hint at color/smoke; flavor will differ.
Crayfish substitutes: dried shrimp powder or a small amount of fish sauce can provide umami (use sparingly).

mealTemplates.label: Egusi-style soup + swallow

components.notes: Pepper-onion oil base is common; some households start from red stew base.

prepNotes: Make pepper base and protein stock ahead for fast weeknight finish.
Add greens at the end to preserve color and freshness.

mealTemplates.label: Jollof rice (party-style) + protein + plantain

components.notes: Jollof typically uses both a pepper-tomato blend and fried tomato paste.

prepNotes: For smoky party notes: finish in oven and allow slight bottom caramelization (controlled).
Batch the pepper blend and freeze in portions.

safetyNotes.label: Dried seafood allergens
notes: Crayfish, dried shrimp, and stockfish are common allergen triggers; tag dishes clearly when serving groups.
Substitute with non-seafood umami (mushroom powder or extra meat stock) if needed.

safetyNotes.label: Palm oil heating
notes: Do not overheat palm oil to smoking; keep medium heat and stir aromatics to prevent scorching.
If palm oil smells burnt, discard and restart to avoid bitterness.

safetyNotes.label: Pepper fumes
notes: Blending or frying hot peppers can irritate eyes/throat; ventilate and consider gloves.
For kid-friendly meals, keep heat add-ons on the side.

availabilityNotes.note: Palm oil is signature; if unavailable use a neutral oil and lean more on crayfish/stock for depth.
Crayfish powder is common; dried shrimp powder or a tiny amount of fish sauce can approximate umami.
Ugu (fluted pumpkin) is a signature green; spinach is the best mainstream substitute.

hallmarkProcesses.name: Pepper base reduction
hallmarkProcesses.notes: Blend peppers + onion, then cook down in oil until reduced, sweeter, and less raw.

hallmarkProcesses.name: Nigerian red stew build
hallmarkProcesses.notes: Fry tomato paste, add pepper base, simmer with proteins/stock until oil separates slightly and stew is thick.

hallmarkProcesses.name: Greens finish (efo-style)
hallmarkProcesses.notes: Add greens near end to keep bright color and fresh bite.

signatureMethods: Reduce pepper base until raw bite is gone and sweetness emerges.
Fry tomato paste briefly to remove raw metallic taste before adding liquids.
Finish greens late; avoid overcooking leafy vegetables.

notes: A blender is highly recommended for pepper bases and jollof sauce.
A heavy-bottom pot reduces scorching during long simmer and reduction.

diet.low_carb.notes: Favor soups/greens/proteins; replace swallows with cauliflower fufu or extra vegetables where desired.

diet.dairy_free.notes: Most traditional dishes are dairy-light; watch modern fusion sauces.

diet.gluten_free.notes: Many swallows are naturally gluten-free; verify semolina/wheat-based options and bouillon ingredients.

core.name: Jollof Rice
core.notes: Tomato-pepper rice; party-style often has smoky notes.

core.name: Nigerian Red Stew
core.notes: Pepper-tomato base reduced in oil; served with rice, yam, plantain, or used to build other dishes.

core.name: Egusi Soup
core.notes: Ground seed thickener with pepper base, proteins, and greens.

core.name: Goat Pepper Soup
core.notes: Aromatic, restorative soup using pepper soup spice blend.

core.name: Suya
core.notes: Spiced grilled meat with peanut-based suya spice; served with onions and pepper sauce.

dessertsAndDrinks.name: Zobo (Hibiscus Drink)
dessertsAndDrinks.notes: Hibiscus drink often with ginger and citrus.

dessertsAndDrinks.name: Puff Puff
dessertsAndDrinks.notes: Fried dough snack; sweetness level varies by household.

typicalFlavorPairings.note: Suya is commonly served with raw onions/tomatoes and pepper sauce; citrus brightens richness.
Egusi soups lean on dried seafood umami and greens for balance.

antiPairings.note: Heavy dairy can dull pepper base brightness; use small yogurt/creamy elements only in modern fusion contexts.

ingredientRules.notes: Palm oil adds signature color/flavor; neutral oils work but reduce 'authentic' signal. Consider smoked fish or paprika for depth.
Use fish sauce sparingly; dried shrimp powder is closest.
Spinach is the most common diaspora substitute; add in the last minutes to avoid mush.
Habanero approximates scotch bonnet heat/aroma; serrano is milder.

methodRules.notes: Use a wire rack on a sheet pan at high heat; finish under broiler for char.
Finely chop peppers/onion/tomato; cook longer to break down; texture will be chunkier but still flavorful.

batchFriendly.name: Pepper base (blended peppers + onion) — reduced
batchFriendly.notes: Freeze in 1-cup portions; foundation for red stew, jollof, and many soups.

batchFriendly.name: Red stew base (tomato paste + pepper base) — reduced
batchFriendly.notes: Batch once; remix throughout the week with different proteins and sides.

batchFriendly.name: Jollof sauce base (pepper-tomato) — reduced
batchFriendly.notes: Use for jollof rice, party rice, or as a stew starter.

batchFriendly.name: Goat/Beef stock with aromatics
batchFriendly.notes: Portion and freeze; speeds up egusi/okra/vegetable soups and deepens flavor.

weeknightShortcuts: Use frozen reduced pepper base + pre-cooked protein to finish a stew in 20–25 minutes.
Cook rice in one pot while stew reheats; fry plantain in a quick pan as a side.
Add greens at the end (3–5 minutes) to keep bright color and reduce overcooking.

notes: Use normalization/aliasing in cuisine rulesets to map local ingredient names (ugwu/ugu, iru, ogbono, etc.) to these IDs.
This profile is designed to support SSA planners, recipe adaptation, and pantry tier suggestions.
